story:
Magic was ruining Ariana’s life. She scowled at the cherry blossoms, the chirping birds, the green fields, and most of all the royal summons in her pocket: the King “requested the pleasure of her company.” Most girls would have swooned; Ariana, born with silver—the rarest, most powerful magic—knew better. Silver was a cheat. Ask for a silk dress, get one muddy boot. Ask for lamb stew, get trout (and she hated fish). It gave her everything except what she asked for. She tore up the letter, stomped it, and went anyway.

Far away in another kind of night, Amy stared at 2:31 A.M. on a hospital clock and breathed in the greasy fog of cafeteria chili fries. If being good at tests had a smell, this was it. She hadn’t become a doctor for love of sleepless shifts; she had just kept getting answers right. Now she regretted every score.

At the palace gate, Ariana hesitated. Kings don’t summon for nothing. Queen? Please. She was all freckles, skinny arms, curly hair—more horse than fairy-tale. He wanted her Gift. Her neighbor Kate, blessed with green magic, grew ears of sugar-snap corn, had a fiancé (the blacksmith’s son) and a baby on the way. Sweet enough, though he’d probably go soft in a few years. Ariana wasn’t jealous. The guards ushered her in.

“Code Blue. Adult Code Blue.” Amy’s pager erupted. She sprinted up stairs, beat the elevator, burst into a room where a nurse’s compressions were too slow, too gentle. Amy took over—hard, fast. Ribs cracked. Vomit slicked her hands. Worse than chili fries. The patient was seventy-two, his mind and body shattered by a stroke. Amy felt that tug between training and mercy. Still, she worked. She counted. She searched for a pulse that didn’t want to come back.

Ariana faced the Prince. He was beautiful in a wintry way: hollow cheeks, pale skin, fever-damp and still. The King, old and tottering, begged. Real wizards had tried and failed; she was the last, wild chance. Ariana sent him out, intending to run. But she stayed. She touched the Prince’s cool, elegant hand and, to the stubborn magic that ruined dinners and dresses, whispered, Are you listening?

In the code room, Amy reached for the quiet center inside her. Her hands did what training demanded, but her heart wished the man peace. If this was his time, let it be gentle.

Ariana reached into that same deep place and wished the Prince life.

“Time of death,” Amy said at last—calm, compassionate. She had done all she could. In her mind’s eye, she saw the man’s spirit rise, unburdened, and drift away. She didn’t rejoice at death; she honored the release.

“And then?” Kate asked later, hungry for romance.

“He woke up,” Ariana said.

“The Prince? Did he kiss you?”

Ariana snorted. Before he could speak, she’d swung out the window and disappeared. No destiny-laden kiss. No crown. The magic, for once, had answered the right wish. That was enough.

“Crazy,” Kate laughed.

“Trust me,” Ariana said, eyeing fields and ordinary chores with cautious hope. “The last thing I need is a Prince.”
